{{short description|Major Japanese clan of samurai}} {{redirect|Taira}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Taira |surname nihongo = 平氏 |home province = [[Hitachi Province]], [[Ise Province]] |image = Ageha-cho.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = [[Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''[[Swallowtail butterfly|Agehachō]]'', the Swallowtail butterfly |parent house = [[Imperial House of Japan]]
([[Emperor Kanmu]]) |titles = Various |founder = [[Taira no Takamochi]] |final ruler = [[Taira no Munemori]] |founding year = {{circa|825}} |ending year = 1185 AD |cadet branches =[[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]]
[[Chiba clan|Chiba]]
[[Miura clan|Miura]]
[[Nagao clan|Nagao]]
[[Uchima]]
[[Tajiri clan|Tajiri]]
[[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]]
[[Oda clan|Oda]]
[[Tanegashima clan|Tanegashima]]
others }} The {{Nihongo|'''Taira'''|平}} was one of the four most important [[Japanese clans|clans]] that dominated Japanese politics during the [[Heian period|Heian]] period of [[History of Japan|Japanese history]] – the others being the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]], and the [[Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]].{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1984|at=Shisei: "Heishi"|oclc=47462068}} The clan is divided into four major groups, named after the [[Emperor of Japan|emperors]] they descended from: [[Emperor Kanmu|Kanmu]] Heishi, [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] Heishi, [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] Heishi, and [[Emperor Kōkō|Kōkō]] Heishi,{{Cite book|title=Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1972|location=Japan|at=Heishi|oclc=38097358}} the most influential of which was the Kanmu Heishi line. In the twilight of the Heian period, the Taira controlled the boy emperor [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] (himself the grandson of the powerful ''[[Kugyō]]'' [[Taira no Kiyomori]]) and had effectively dominated the Imperial capital of [[Heian-kyō|Heian]]. However, they were opposed by their rivals the [[Minamoto clan]] (the Genji), which culminated in the [[Genpei War]] (1180–1185 AD). The five-year-long war concluded with a decisive Taira defeat in the naval [[Battle of Dan-no-Ura]], which resulted in the deaths of Antoku and Taira leaders. Following the war, the victorious Minamoto established Japan's [[Kamakura shogunate|first shogunate]] in [[Kamakura]]. The name "Genpei" comes from [[On'yomi|alternate readings]] of the ''[[kanji]]'' "Minamoto" (源 ''Gen'') and "Taira" (平 ''Hei''). The clan is commonly referred to as {{nihongo|'''Heishi'''|平氏||"Taira clan"}} or {{nihongo|'''Heike'''|[[wikt:平家|平家]]||"House of Taira"}}, using the character's [[Kanji#On'yomi (Chinese reading)|On'yomi]] {{nihongo|''hei''|平}} for ''Taira'', while {{nihongo|''shi''|氏}} means "[[Uji (clan)|clan]]", and {{nihongo|''ke''|家}} is used as a suffix for "[[extended family]]".{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xa0wDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA72|title=Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility|last=Lebra|first=Takie Sugiyama|date=1995|publisher=University of California Press, p. 72|language=en|isbn=9780520076020}} The clan is the namesake of ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', an epic account of the Genpei War. ==History== [[File:Map-of-Japan-1183-Heian-Genpei-War.png|thumb|The domain of the Taira clan in Japan (1183)]] [[File:Taira-Clan-Warriors-Ukiyoe-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png|thumb|Warriors of the Taira clan by [[Utagawa Yoshitora]]]] Along with the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], Taira was one of the honorary surnames given by the emperors of the [[Heian period]] (794–1185) to their children and grandchildren who were not considered eligible for the throne. The clan was founded when the [[Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]] grew too large, and the emperor ordered that the descendants of previous emperors from several generations ago would no longer be princes but would instead be given noble surnames and ranks. The decision became applicable during the reign of [[Emperor Kanmu]] (782–805) and thus, together with the Minamoto clan, the Taira clan was born.[http://samurai-archives.com/HeianPeriod.html Samurai Archives] Some grandchildren of [[Emperor Kanmu]] were the first to bear the name of Taira, after [[825]]. Later, descendants of [[Emperor Nimmyo]], [[Emperor Montoku]] and [[Emperor Koko]] also received the surname. The specific hereditary lines of these emperors are referred to by the posthumous name of the emperor followed by Heishi, for example ''Kanmu Heishi''.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA112|title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context|last=Plutschow|first=Herbert E.|date=1995|publisher=Psychology Press|pages=112–113|language=en|isbn=9781873410424}} The Kanmu Heishi line has two major branches. One was founded in [[889]] by [[Taira no Takamochi]] (great-grandson of the 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]], who reigned from [[781]] to [[806]]) proved to be the strongest and most dominant line during the [[Heian period]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TBkqqPMgUuwC&pg=PA9|title=Warriors of Japan: As Portrayed in the War Tales|last=Varley|first=H. Paul|date=1994|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|page=9|language=en|isbn=9780824816018}} A great-grandson of Takamochi, [[Taira no Korehira]], moved to [[Ise Province]] (currently part of [[Mie Prefecture]]) and established an important [[Daimyo]] dynasty.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=66gCAwAAQBAJ&pg=PP7|title=Un vassallo che cercò di espugnare la Dinastia|last=Zumbo|first=Daniele|date=2013|publisher=Youcanprint|page=7|language=it|isbn=9788891113221}} [[Taira no Masamori|Masamori]], his grandson; and [[Taira no Tadamori|Tadamori]], his great-grandson, became loyal supporters of [[Emperor Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba]], respectively. Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Taira clan| ]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Taira clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]]

One was founded in [[889]] by [[Taira no Takamochi]] (great-grandson of the 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]], who reigned from [[781]] to [[806]]) proved to be the strongest and most dominant line during the [[Heian period]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TBkqqPMgUuwC&pg=PA9|title=Warriors of Japan: As Portrayed in the War Tales|last=Varley|first=H. Paul|date=1994|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|page=9|language=en|isbn=9780824816018}} A great-grandson of Takamochi, [[Taira no Korehira]], moved to [[Ise Province]] (currently part of [[Mie Prefecture]]) and established an important [[Daimyo]] dynasty.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=66gCAwAAQBAJ&pg=PP7|title=Un vassallo che cercò di espugnare la Dinastia|last=Zumbo|first=Daniele|date=2013|publisher=Youcanprint|page=7|language=it|isbn=9788891113221}} [[Taira no Masamori|Masamori]], his grandson; and [[Taira no Tadamori|Tadamori]], his great-grandson, became loyal supporters of [[Emperor Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba]], respectively. Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Taira clan| ]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Taira clan]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]]

{{short description|Major Japanese clan of samurai}} {{redirect|Taira}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Taira |surname nihongo = 平氏 |home province = [[Hitachi Province]], [[Ise Province]] |image = Ageha-cho.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = [[Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''[[Swallowtail butterfly|Agehachō]]'', the Swallowtail butterfly |parent house = [[Imperial House of Japan]]
([[Emperor Kanmu]]) |titles = Various |founder = [[Taira no Takamochi]] |final ruler = [[Taira no Munemori]] |founding year = {{circa|825}} |ending year = 1185 AD |cadet branches =[[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]]
[[Chiba clan|Chiba]]
[[Miura clan|Miura]]
[[Nagao clan|Nagao]]
[[Uchima]]
[[Tajiri clan|Tajiri]]
[[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]]
[[Oda clan|Oda]]
[[Tanegashima clan|Tanegashima]]
others }} The {{Nihongo|'''Taira'''|平}} was one of the four most important [[Japanese clans|clans]] that dominated Japanese politics during the [[Heian period|Heian]] period of [[History of Japan|Japanese history]] – the others being the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]], and the [[Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]].{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1984|at=Shisei: "Heishi"|oclc=47462068}} The clan is divided into four major groups, named after the [[Emperor of Japan|emperors]] they descended from: [[Emperor Kanmu|Kanmu]] Heishi, [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] Heishi, [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] Heishi, and [[Emperor Kōkō|Kōkō]] Heishi,{{Cite book|title=Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1972|location=Japan|at=Heishi|oclc=38097358}} the most influential of which was the Kanmu Heishi line. In the twilight of the Heian period, the Taira controlled the boy emperor [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] (himself the grandson of the powerful ''[[Kugyō]]'' [[Taira no Kiyomori]]) and had effectively dominated the Imperial capital of [[Heian-kyō|Heian]]. However, they were opposed by their rivals the [[Minamoto clan]] (the Genji), which culminated in the [[Genpei War]] (1180–1185 AD). The five-year-long war concluded with a decisive Taira defeat in the naval [[Battle of Dan-no-Ura]], which resulted in the deaths of Antoku and Taira leaders. Following the war, the victorious Minamoto established Japan's [[Kamakura shogunate|first shogunate]] in [[Kamakura]]. The name "Genpei" comes from [[On'yomi|alternate readings]] of the ''[[kanji]]'' "Minamoto" (源 ''Gen'') and "Taira" (平 ''Hei''). The clan is commonly referred to as {{nihongo|'''Heishi'''|平氏||"Taira clan"}} or {{nihongo|'''Heike'''|[[wikt:平家|平家]]||"House of Taira"}}, using the character's [[Kanji#On'yomi (Chinese reading)|On'yomi]] {{nihongo|''hei''|平}} for ''Taira'', while {{nihongo|''shi''|氏}} means "[[Uji (clan)|clan]]", and {{nihongo|''ke''|家}} is used as a suffix for "[[extended family]]".{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xa0wDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA72|title=Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility|last=Lebra|first=Takie Sugiyama|date=1995|publisher=University of California Press, p. 72|language=en|isbn=9780520076020}} The clan is the namesake of ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', an epic account of the Genpei War. ==History== [[File:Map-of-Japan-1183-Heian-Genpei-War.png|thumb|The domain of the Taira clan in Japan (1183)]] [[File:Taira-Clan-Warriors-Ukiyoe-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png|thumb|Warriors of the Taira clan by [[Utagawa Yoshitora]]]] Along with the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], Taira was one of the honorary surnames given by the emperors of the [[Heian period]] (794–1185) to their children and grandchildren who were not considered eligible for the throne. The clan was founded when the [[Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]] grew too large, and the emperor ordered that the descendants of previous emperors from several generations ago would no longer be princes but would instead be given noble surnames and ranks. The decision became applicable during the reign of [[Emperor Kanmu]] (782–805) and thus, together with the Minamoto clan, the Taira clan was born.[http://samurai-archives.com/HeianPeriod.html Samurai Archives] Some grandchildren of [[Emperor Kanmu]] were the first to bear the name of Taira, after [[825]]. Later, descendants of [[Emperor Nimmyo]], [[Emperor Montoku]] and [[Emperor Koko]] also received the surname. The specific hereditary lines of these emperors are referred to by the posthumous name of the emperor followed by Heishi, for example ''Kanmu Heishi''.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA112|title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context|last=Plutschow|first=Herbert E.|date=1995|publisher=Psychology Press|pages=112–113|language=en|isbn=9781873410424}} The Kanmu Heishi line has two major branches. One was founded in [[889]] by [[Taira no Takamochi]] (great-grandson of the 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]], who reigned from [[781]] to [[806]]) proved to be the strongest and most dominant line during the [[Heian period]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TBkqqPMgUuwC&pg=PA9|title=Warriors of Japan: As Portrayed in the War Tales|last=Varley|first=H. Paul|date=1994|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|page=9|language=en|isbn=9780824816018}} A great-grandson of Takamochi, [[Taira no Korehira]], moved to [[Ise Province]] (currently part of [[Mie Prefecture]]) and established an important [[Daimyo]] dynasty.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=66gCAwAAQBAJ&pg=PP7|title=Un vassallo che cercò di espugnare la Dinastia|last=Zumbo|first=Daniele|date=2013|publisher=Youcanprint|page=7|language=it|isbn=9788891113221}} [[Taira no Masamori|Masamori]], his grandson; and [[Taira no Tadamori|Tadamori]], his great-grandson, became loyal supporters of [[Emperor Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba]], respectively. Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Taira clan| ]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] ([[Emperor Kanmu]]) |titles = Various |founder = [[Taira no Takamochi]] |final ruler = [[Taira no Munemori]] |founding year = {{circa|825}} |ending year = 1185 AD |cadet branches =[[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]]

Paul|date=1994|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|page=9|language=en|isbn=9780824816018}} A great-grandson of Takamochi, [[Taira no Korehira]], moved to [[Ise Province]] (currently part of [[Mie Prefecture]]) and established an important [[Daimyo]] dynasty.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=66gCAwAAQBAJ&pg=PP7|title=Un vassallo che cercò di espugnare la Dinastia|last=Zumbo|first=Daniele|date=2013|publisher=Youcanprint|page=7|language=it|isbn=9788891113221}} [[Taira no Masamori|Masamori]], his grandson; and [[Taira no Tadamori|Tadamori]], his great-grandson, became loyal supporters of [[Emperor Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba]], respectively. Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[category:kk]][[category:Taira clan| ]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese clans]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Taira clan]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]]

{{short description|Major Japanese clan of samurai}} {{redirect|Taira}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Taira |surname nihongo = 平氏 |home province = [[Hitachi Province]], [[Ise Province]] |image = Ageha-cho.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = [[Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''[[Swallowtail butterfly|Agehachō]]'', the Swallowtail butterfly |parent house = [[Imperial House of Japan]]
([[Emperor Kanmu]]) |titles = Various |founder = [[Taira no Takamochi]] |final ruler = [[Taira no Munemori]] |founding year = {{circa|825}} |ending year = 1185 AD |cadet branches =[[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]]
[[Chiba clan|Chiba]]
[[Miura clan|Miura]]
[[Nagao clan|Nagao]]
[[Uchima]]
[[Tajiri clan|Tajiri]]
[[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]]
[[Oda clan|Oda]]
[[Tanegashima clan|Tanegashima]]
others }} The {{Nihongo|'''Taira'''|平}} was one of the four most important [[Japanese clans|clans]] that dominated Japanese politics during the [[Heian period|Heian]] period of [[History of Japan|Japanese history]] – the others being the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]], and the [[Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]].{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1984|at=Shisei: "Heishi"|oclc=47462068}} The clan is divided into four major groups, named after the [[Emperor of Japan|emperors]] they descended from: [[Emperor Kanmu|Kanmu]] Heishi, [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] Heishi, [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] Heishi, and [[Emperor Kōkō|Kōkō]] Heishi,{{Cite book|title=Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1972|location=Japan|at=Heishi|oclc=38097358}} the most influential of which was the Kanmu Heishi line. In the twilight of the Heian period, the Taira controlled the boy emperor [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] (himself the grandson of the powerful ''[[Kugyō]]'' [[Taira no Kiyomori]]) and had effectively dominated the Imperial capital of [[Heian-kyō|Heian]]. However, they were opposed by their rivals the [[Minamoto clan]] (the Genji), which culminated in the [[Genpei War]] (1180–1185 AD). The five-year-long war concluded with a decisive Taira defeat in the naval [[Battle of Dan-no-Ura]], which resulted in the deaths of Antoku and Taira leaders. Following the war, the victorious Minamoto established Japan's [[Kamakura shogunate|first shogunate]] in [[Kamakura]]. The name "Genpei" comes from [[On'yomi|alternate readings]] of the ''[[kanji]]'' "Minamoto" (源 ''Gen'') and "Taira" (平 ''Hei''). The clan is commonly referred to as {{nihongo|'''Heishi'''|平氏||"Taira clan"}} or {{nihongo|'''Heike'''|[[wikt:平家|平家]]||"House of Taira"}}, using the character's [[Kanji#On'yomi (Chinese reading)|On'yomi]] {{nihongo|''hei''|平}} for ''Taira'', while {{nihongo|''shi''|氏}} means "[[Uji (clan)|clan]]", and {{nihongo|''ke''|家}} is used as a suffix for "[[extended family]]".{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xa0wDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA72|title=Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility|last=Lebra|first=Takie Sugiyama|date=1995|publisher=University of California Press, p. 72|language=en|isbn=9780520076020}} The clan is the namesake of ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', an epic account of the Genpei War. ==History== [[File:Map-of-Japan-1183-Heian-Genpei-War.png|thumb|The domain of the Taira clan in Japan (1183)]] [[File:Taira-Clan-Warriors-Ukiyoe-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png|thumb|Warriors of the Taira clan by [[Utagawa Yoshitora]]]] Along with the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], Taira was one of the honorary surnames given by the emperors of the [[Heian period]] (794–1185) to their children and grandchildren who were not considered eligible for the throne. The clan was founded when the [[Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]] grew too large, and the emperor ordered that the descendants of previous emperors from several generations ago would no longer be princes but would instead be given noble surnames and ranks. The decision became applicable during the reign of [[Emperor Kanmu]] (782–805) and thus, together with the Minamoto clan, the Taira clan was born.[http://samurai-archives.com/HeianPeriod.html Samurai Archives] Some grandchildren of [[Emperor Kanmu]] were the first to bear the name of Taira, after [[825]]. Later, descendants of [[Emperor Nimmyo]], [[Emperor Montoku]] and [[Emperor Koko]] also received the surname. The specific hereditary lines of these emperors are referred to by the posthumous name of the emperor followed by Heishi, for example ''Kanmu Heishi''.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA112|title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context|last=Plutschow|first=Herbert E.|date=1995|publisher=Psychology Press|pages=112–113|language=en|isbn=9781873410424}} The Kanmu Heishi line has two major branches. One was founded in [[889]] by [[Taira no Takamochi]] (great-grandson of the 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]], who reigned from [[781]] to [[806]]) proved to be the strongest and most dominant line during the [[Heian period]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TBkqqPMgUuwC&pg=PA9|title=Warriors of Japan: As Portrayed in the War Tales|last=Varley|first=H. Paul|date=1994|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|page=9|language=en|isbn=9780824816018}} A great-grandson of Takamochi, [[Taira no Korehira]], moved to [[Ise Province]] (currently part of [[Mie Prefecture]]) and established an important [[Daimyo]] dynasty.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=66gCAwAAQBAJ&pg=PP7|title=Un vassallo che cercò di espugnare la Dinastia|last=Zumbo|first=Daniele|date=2013|publisher=Youcanprint|page=7|language=it|isbn=9788891113221}} [[Taira no Masamori|Masamori]], his grandson; and [[Taira no Tadamori|Tadamori]], his great-grandson, became loyal supporters of [[Emperor Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba]], respectively. Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[category:kk]][[category:Taira clan| ]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese clans]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[category:kk]][[category:Taira clan| ]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese clans]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Taira clan]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:ファイル]]

{{short description|Major Japanese clan of samurai}} {{redirect|Taira}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Taira |surname nihongo = 平氏 |home province = [[Hitachi Province]], [[Ise Province]] |image = Ageha-cho.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = [[Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''[[Swallowtail butterfly|Agehachō]]'', the Swallowtail butterfly |parent house = [[Imperial House of Japan]]
([[Emperor Kanmu]]) |titles = Various |founder = [[Taira no Takamochi]] |final ruler = [[Taira no Munemori]] |founding year = {{circa|825}} |ending year = 1185 AD |cadet branches =[[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]]
[[Chiba clan|Chiba]]
[[Miura clan|Miura]]
[[Nagao clan|Nagao]]
[[Uchima]]
[[Tajiri clan|Tajiri]]
[[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]]
[[Oda clan|Oda]]
[[Tanegashima clan|Tanegashima]]
others }} The {{Nihongo|'''Taira'''|平}} was one of the four most important [[Japanese clans|clans]] that dominated Japanese politics during the [[Heian period|Heian]] period of [[History of Japan|Japanese history]] – the others being the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]], and the [[Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]].{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1984|at=Shisei: "Heishi"|oclc=47462068}} The clan is divided into four major groups, named after the [[Emperor of Japan|emperors]] they descended from: [[Emperor Kanmu|Kanmu]] Heishi, [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] Heishi, [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] Heishi, and [[Emperor Kōkō|Kōkō]] Heishi,{{Cite book|title=Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1972|location=Japan|at=Heishi|oclc=38097358}} the most influential of which was the Kanmu Heishi line. In the twilight of the Heian period, the Taira controlled the boy emperor [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] (himself the grandson of the powerful ''[[Kugyō]]'' [[Taira no Kiyomori]]) and had effectively dominated the Imperial capital of [[Heian-kyō|Heian]]. However, they were opposed by their rivals the [[Minamoto clan]] (the Genji), which culminated in the [[Genpei War]] (1180–1185 AD). The five-year-long war concluded with a decisive Taira defeat in the naval [[Battle of Dan-no-Ura]], which resulted in the deaths of Antoku and Taira leaders. Following the war, the victorious Minamoto established Japan's [[Kamakura shogunate|first shogunate]] in [[Kamakura]]. The name "Genpei" comes from [[On'yomi|alternate readings]] of the ''[[kanji]]'' "Minamoto" (源 ''Gen'') and "Taira" (平 ''Hei''). The clan is commonly referred to as {{nihongo|'''Heishi'''|平氏||"Taira clan"}} or {{nihongo|'''Heike'''|[[wikt:平家|平家]]||"House of Taira"}}, using the character's [[Kanji#On'yomi (Chinese reading)|On'yomi]] {{nihongo|''hei''|平}} for ''Taira'', while {{nihongo|''shi''|氏}} means "[[Uji (clan)|clan]]", and {{nihongo|''ke''|家}} is used as a suffix for "[[extended family]]".{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xa0wDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA72|title=Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility|last=Lebra|first=Takie Sugiyama|date=1995|publisher=University of California Press, p. 72|language=en|isbn=9780520076020}} The clan is the namesake of ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', an epic account of the Genpei War. ==History== [[File:Map-of-Japan-1183-Heian-Genpei-War.png|thumb|The domain of the Taira clan in Japan (1183)]] [[File:Taira-Clan-Warriors-Ukiyoe-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png|thumb|Warriors of the Taira clan by [[Utagawa Yoshitora]]]] Along with the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], Taira was one of the honorary surnames given by the emperors of the [[Heian period]] (794–1185) to their children and grandchildren who were not considered eligible for the throne. The clan was founded when the [[Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]] grew too large, and the emperor ordered that the descendants of previous emperors from several generations ago would no longer be princes but would instead be given noble surnames and ranks. The decision became applicable during the reign of [[Emperor Kanmu]] (782–805) and thus, together with the Minamoto clan, the Taira clan was born.[http://samurai-archives.com/HeianPeriod.html Samurai Archives] Some grandchildren of [[Emperor Kanmu]] were the first to bear the name of Taira, after [[825]]. Later, descendants of [[Emperor Nimmyo]], [[Emperor Montoku]] and [[Emperor Koko]] also received the surname. The specific hereditary lines of these emperors are referred to by the posthumous name of the emperor followed by Heishi, for example ''Kanmu Heishi''.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA112|title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context|last=Plutschow|first=Herbert E.|date=1995|publisher=Psychology Press|pages=112–113|language=en|isbn=9781873410424}} The Kanmu Heishi line has two major branches. One was founded in [[889]] by [[Taira no Takamochi]] (great-grandson of the 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]], who reigned from [[781]] to [[806]]) proved to be the strongest and most dominant line during the [[Heian period]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TBkqqPMgUuwC&pg=PA9|title=Warriors of Japan: As Portrayed in the War Tales|last=Varley|first=H. Paul|date=1994|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|page=9|language=en|isbn=9780824816018}} A great-grandson of Takamochi, [[Taira no Korehira]], moved to [[Ise Province]] (currently part of [[Mie Prefecture]]) and established an important [[Daimyo]] dynasty.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=66gCAwAAQBAJ&pg=PP7|title=Un vassallo che cercò di espugnare la Dinastia|last=Zumbo|first=Daniele|date=2013|publisher=Youcanprint|page=7|language=it|isbn=9788891113221}} [[Taira no Masamori|Masamori]], his grandson; and [[Taira no Tadamori|Tadamori]], his great-grandson, became loyal supporters of [[Emperor Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba]], respectively. Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[category:kk]][[category:Taira clan| ]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese clans]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taira clan]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Taira clan]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taira clan]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:ファイル]]

Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[category:kk]][[category:Taira clan| ]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese clans]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taira clan]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Taira clan]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taira clan]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:ファイル]]

{{short description|Major Japanese clan of samurai}} {{redirect|Taira}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Taira |surname nihongo = 平氏 |home province = [[Hitachi Province]], [[Ise Province]] |image = Ageha-cho.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = [[Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''[[Swallowtail butterfly|Agehachō]]'', the Swallowtail butterfly |parent house = [[Imperial House of Japan]]
([[Emperor Kanmu]]) |titles = Various |founder = [[Taira no Takamochi]] |final ruler = [[Taira no Munemori]] |founding year = {{circa|825}} |ending year = 1185 AD |cadet branches =[[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]]
[[Chiba clan|Chiba]]
[[Miura clan|Miura]]
[[Nagao clan|Nagao]]
[[Uchima]]
[[Tajiri clan|Tajiri]]
[[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]]
[[Oda clan|Oda]]
[[Tanegashima clan|Tanegashima]]
others }} The {{Nihongo|'''Taira'''|平}} was one of the four most important [[Japanese clans|clans]] that dominated Japanese politics during the [[Heian period|Heian]] period of [[History of Japan|Japanese history]] – the others being the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]], and the [[Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]].{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1984|at=Shisei: "Heishi"|oclc=47462068}} The clan is divided into four major groups, named after the [[Emperor of Japan|emperors]] they descended from: [[Emperor Kanmu|Kanmu]] Heishi, [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] Heishi, [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] Heishi, and [[Emperor Kōkō|Kōkō]] Heishi,{{Cite book|title=Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1972|location=Japan|at=Heishi|oclc=38097358}} the most influential of which was the Kanmu Heishi line. In the twilight of the Heian period, the Taira controlled the boy emperor [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] (himself the grandson of the powerful ''[[Kugyō]]'' [[Taira no Kiyomori]]) and had effectively dominated the Imperial capital of [[Heian-kyō|Heian]]. However, they were opposed by their rivals the [[Minamoto clan]] (the Genji), which culminated in the [[Genpei War]] (1180–1185 AD). The five-year-long war concluded with a decisive Taira defeat in the naval [[Battle of Dan-no-Ura]], which resulted in the deaths of Antoku and Taira leaders. Following the war, the victorious Minamoto established Japan's [[Kamakura shogunate|first shogunate]] in [[Kamakura]]. The name "Genpei" comes from [[On'yomi|alternate readings]] of the ''[[kanji]]'' "Minamoto" (源 ''Gen'') and "Taira" (平 ''Hei''). The clan is commonly referred to as {{nihongo|'''Heishi'''|平氏||"Taira clan"}} or {{nihongo|'''Heike'''|[[wikt:平家|平家]]||"House of Taira"}}, using the character's [[Kanji#On'yomi (Chinese reading)|On'yomi]] {{nihongo|''hei''|平}} for ''Taira'', while {{nihongo|''shi''|氏}} means "[[Uji (clan)|clan]]", and {{nihongo|''ke''|家}} is used as a suffix for "[[extended family]]".{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xa0wDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA72|title=Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility|last=Lebra|first=Takie Sugiyama|date=1995|publisher=University of California Press, p. 72|language=en|isbn=9780520076020}} The clan is the namesake of ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', an epic account of the Genpei War. ==History== [[File:Map-of-Japan-1183-Heian-Genpei-War.png|thumb|The domain of the Taira clan in Japan (1183)]] [[File:Taira-Clan-Warriors-Ukiyoe-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png|thumb|Warriors of the Taira clan by [[Utagawa Yoshitora]]]] Along with the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]], Taira was one of the honorary surnames given by the emperors of the [[Heian period]] (794–1185) to their children and grandchildren who were not considered eligible for the throne. The clan was founded when the [[Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]] grew too large, and the emperor ordered that the descendants of previous emperors from several generations ago would no longer be princes but would instead be given noble surnames and ranks. The decision became applicable during the reign of [[Emperor Kanmu]] (782–805) and thus, together with the Minamoto clan, the Taira clan was born.[http://samurai-archives.com/HeianPeriod.html Samurai Archives] Some grandchildren of [[Emperor Kanmu]] were the first to bear the name of Taira, after [[825]]. Later, descendants of [[Emperor Nimmyo]], [[Emperor Montoku]] and [[Emperor Koko]] also received the surname. The specific hereditary lines of these emperors are referred to by the posthumous name of the emperor followed by Heishi, for example ''Kanmu Heishi''.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA112|title=Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context|last=Plutschow|first=Herbert E.|date=1995|publisher=Psychology Press|pages=112–113|language=en|isbn=9781873410424}} The Kanmu Heishi line has two major branches. One was founded in [[889]] by [[Taira no Takamochi]] (great-grandson of the 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]], who reigned from [[781]] to [[806]]) proved to be the strongest and most dominant line during the [[Heian period]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TBkqqPMgUuwC&pg=PA9|title=Warriors of Japan: As Portrayed in the War Tales|last=Varley|first=H. Paul|date=1994|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|page=9|language=en|isbn=9780824816018}} A great-grandson of Takamochi, [[Taira no Korehira]], moved to [[Ise Province]] (currently part of [[Mie Prefecture]]) and established an important [[Daimyo]] dynasty.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=66gCAwAAQBAJ&pg=PP7|title=Un vassallo che cercò di espugnare la Dinastia|last=Zumbo|first=Daniele|date=2013|publisher=Youcanprint|page=7|language=it|isbn=9788891113221}} [[Taira no Masamori|Masamori]], his grandson; and [[Taira no Tadamori|Tadamori]], his great-grandson, became loyal supporters of [[Emperor Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba]], respectively. Later, Tadamori's son, [[Taira no Kiyomori]], created what was considered the first [[samurai]] government in the history of Japan.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OF4aAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA176|title=The Tales of the Heike|last1=Watson|first1=Burton|last2=Shirane|first2=Haruo|date=2006|publisher=Columbia University Press|page=176|language=en|isbn=9780231510837}} Taira no Kiyomori, son and heir of Tadamori, rose to the position of [[Daijō Daijin]] (great Minister of State), after his victories in the [[Hōgen rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156) and the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160). Kiyomori succeeded in enthroning his youngest grandson as [[Emperor Antoku]] in 1180, an act that led to the [[Genpei War]] (Genpei no Sōran, 1180–1185). The last leader of the Kanmu Heishi bloodline, was eventually destroyed by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s armies at the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], the last battle of the Genpei War. This story is told in the [[Heike Monogatari]].{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/genjiheikeselect00mccu/page/250 250]|title=Genji & Heike: Selections from The Tale of Genji and The Tale of the Heike|date=1994|publisher=Stanford University Press|language=en|isbn=9780804766463}} This branch of the Kanmu Heishi had many collateral branches, including [[Hojo clan|Hōjō]], [[Chiba clan|Chiba]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JWIUAQAAIAAJ&q=Kanmu+Heishi|title=The Story of Japan: History from the founding of the nation to the height of Fujiwara prosperity|last=Hiraizumi|first=Kiyoshi|date=1997|publisher=Seisei Kikaku p 5|language=en|isbn=9784916079046}} The other major branch of Kanmu Heishi was founded by Takamune-ō (804–867), the eldest son of Prince Imperial Kazurahara and grandson of Emperor Kanmu, who received the title of Taira no [[Ason]] in the year 825.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x5mec8BiZQcC&q=Takamune|title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London|date=1932|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company, p. 105|language=en}} Members of this branch served as middle-class [[kuge]] in the Imperial Court of Kyoto. The [[Oda clan]] at the time of [[Oda Nobunaga]] (1534–1582) also claimed Taira descent, they were descendants of [[Taira no Chikazane]], grandson of [[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179).{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA156|title=Japan's Name Culture|last=Plutschow|year=1995|page=156|publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=9781873410424}} == Genpei War == During the [[Heiji Rebellion]] (1160), the [[Seiwa Genji]] leader, [[Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], died in battle. Taira no Kiyomori gained power in [[Kyoto]] forging alliances with retired emperors [[Emperor Shirakawa|Shirakawa]] and [[Emperor Toba|Toba]]. Kiyomori sent [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199), the third son of Yoshitomo, into exile. In [[1180]], Yoritomo organized a large-scale rebellion against the rule of the Taira (the [[Genpei War]] or ''Taira-Minamoto''), culminated with the destruction of the Taira by the [[Minamoto clan]]{{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=255–257, 275, 289–305}} and the subjugation of eastern Japan in five years. In [[1192]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] received the title [[shogun]] and created the first [[bakufu]] based in [[Kamakura]] (Kanagawa Prefecture).{{Cite news|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/shogun|title=shogun {{!}} Japanese title|work=Encyclopedia Britannica|access-date=2017-08-21|language=en}} == Branches == The Taira clan had four main branches:[http://crustacea.nhm.org/people/martin/publications/pdf/103.pdf The Samurai Crab] *Taira Kanmu (Kanmu Heishi, 桓武平氏) – descended from the princes, children of 50th [[Emperor Kanmu]]. *Taira Nimmyō (Nimmyō Heishi, 仁明平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 54th [[Emperor Nimmyō]]'s lineage. *Taira Montoku (Montoku Heishi, 文徳平氏) – descended from princes, children of 55th [[Emperor Montoku]]. *Taira Kōkō (Kōkō Heishi, 光孝平氏) – descended from the princes, grandchildren of the 58th [[Emperor Kōkō]]'s lineage. == Clan members == These were important members of the Taira clan. *[[Taira no Masakado]] (903–940) *[[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118–1181) *[[Taira no Shigemori]] (1138–1179) *[[Taira no Tadanori]] (1144–1184) *[[Taira no Munemori]] (1147–1185) *[[Taira no Tomomori]] (1152–1185) *[[Taira no Shigehira]] (1158–1185) *[[Taira no Takakiyo]] (1173–1199) == Mon of the Taira == The [[Mon (badge)|''mon'']] (crest, emblem) of the Taira clan is an Agehanochō (揚羽蝶, [[Swallowtail butterfly]]) with raised wings. == Gallery == File:Yoshitsune_aangevallen_door_Taira_geesten-Rijksmuseum_RP-P-1979-177.jpeg|[[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] defending themselves in their boat during a storm created by the ghosts of conquered Taira warriors (by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]) ==See also== * [[Japanese clans]] * [[Japanese name]] * [[Taira no Masakado]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[category:kk]][[category:Taira clan| ]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese clans]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taira clan]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Taira clan]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taira clan]]]] [[category:mm]]
